coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen reduction of porous N-doped carbon nanosheets with graphitic N and defects obtained from coal-based graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Jing
Wang, Kun
Deng, Bangwei
Li, Yizhao
Zhang, Su
Cao, Yali
coredata.dc:description: Metal-free carbon-based electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) have received considerable attention owing to their abundance and low cost. However, designing carbon-based electrocatalysts with excellent performance in a facile manner remains a challenge. In this work, porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheet (PNCNs) catalysts with high contents of graphitic N and defects were readily prepared via a simple self-doping method, in which coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as the carbon and nitrogen resource. Based on the results, PNCNs-900 (obtained at 900 °C) displayed good ORR performance, with a half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.80 V. It also exhibited high stability and methanol tolerance compared with commercial Pt/C. Experimental investigations and theoretical calculations prove that the increased ORR electrocatalytic activity of PNCNs-900 is due to the synergistic effect of a large number of defects and graphitic N. Furthermore, its higher electrocatalytic activity for ORR also benefits from the porous lamellar structure, which facilitates the mass transfer process. This study offers a novel approach for developing effective ORR catalysts and for the high value-added utilization of coal resources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon nanosheet
Graphitic N
Defects
Coal-based graphene quantum dots
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21766036 , 52062046 , and 52100166 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) HRTEM images of GQDs. (d-f) SEM images of PNCNs-900. (g-h) TEM images of PNCNs-900. (i) SEM elements mapping of C, N and O elements of PNCNs-900, respectively.
(a) XRD spectra. (b) Raman spectra of NC-900 and PNCNs-900. (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (d) pore size distributions of the NC-900 and PNCNs-900.

(a) XPS survey scan spectra of different samples. (b) The contents of N and O elemental corresponding to XPS. (c) The high-resolution N 1s, (d) O 1s spectrum of PNCNs-900. (e) the contents of different nitrogen-doped species in samples.

(a) CV curves of PNCNs-900 in N2- and O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. (b) LSV curves and (c) the histogram of E 1/2 and j of NC-900, PNCNs-800, PNCNs-900, PNCNs-1000 and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm. (d) Tafel curves. (e) LSV curves of PNCNs-900 with different rotating speeds. (f) K-L curves of PNCNs-900.

(a) LSV curves in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with rotating speed of 1600 rpm by RRDE. (b) Electron transfer number (n) and (c) H2O2 yield obtained by RRDE. (d) I-t curves obtained by chronoamperometry. (e) LSV curves of PNCNs-900 before and after in cycling. (f) Methanol tolerance curves.
(a) DFT-optimized carbon defect sites with N doping. (b) Calculated Gibbs free-energy diagrams for the ORR pathway of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N dopants at U = 1.23 V.
Schematic description for the preparation of the PNCNs.
Specific surface area of PNCNs measured by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm.
Related concentration of C, N and O in the samples from elemental analysis.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen reduction of porous N-doped carbon nanosheets with graphitic N and defects obtained from coal-based graphene quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-free carbon-based electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) have received considerable attention owing to their abundance and low cost. However, designing carbon-based electrocatalysts with excellent performance in a facile manner remains a challenge. In this work, porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheet (PNCNs) catalysts with high contents of graphitic N and defects were readily prepared via a simple self-doping method, in which coal-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as the carbon and nitrogen resource. Based on the results, PNCNs-900 (obtained at 900 °C) displayed good ORR performance, with a half-wave potential (E 1/2) of 0.80 V. It also exhibited high stability and methanol tolerance compared with commercial Pt/C. Experimental investigations and theoretical calculations prove that the increased ORR electrocatalytic activity of PNCNs-900 is due to the synergistic effect of a large number of defects and graphitic N. Furthermore, its higher electrocatalytic activity for ORR also benefits from the porous lamellar structure, which facilitates the mass transfer process. This study offers a novel approach for developing effective ORR catalysts and for the high value-added utilization of coal resources.

In this work, porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheets (PNCNs) were prepared by a simple template-assisted assembly strategy from coal-based graphene quantum dots. PNCNs-900 (obtained at 900 °C) displays good oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performance and outperforms stability compared with commercial Pt/C. Characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculation displayed that the synergistic effect of large number of defects and graphitic N in PNCNS-900 was responsible for the improvement of ORR activity.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: New types of energy conversion devices, such as fuel cells and metal-air cells, have attracted considerable attention in many fields. However, as the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), which is the cathodic reaction for fuel and metal-air cells, displays slow reaction kinetics and a high reaction energy barrier, it requires catalysts with high activity, selectivity, and stability to increase the reaction rate [1]. Noble metal-based catalysts (such as Pt) are considered the benchmark catalysts for the ORR [2,3]. However, commercial Pt/C catalysts are scarce and expensive for industrial application. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the use as well as performance of non-noble metal catalysts, which are economical compared with Pt/C catalysts, for the ORR. Among the numerous low-cost and non-Pt catalysts, metal-free carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene [4], porous carbon [5], and carbon nanotubes [6], etc.) have received considerable attention. However, the in-plane carbon electron arrangement in pure carbon materials is uniform and lacks reactive sites [7]. Based on previous studies, local charge redistribution through heteroatom doping [8] and defect engineering [9] is an effective strategy for modifying inert carbon materials.

Heteroatom doping (e.g., N doping) can enhance the adsorption capacity of carbon materials for oxygen species by modulating the local electron density by changing electronegativity values [10,11]. In the carbon skeleton, the nitrogen species are classified as pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized N. Generally, pyridinic N and graphitic N are considered as the active for the ORR [12,13]. However, the effect of nitrogen species on catalytic activity remains unclear. Moreover, defects can change the local charge density, resulting in a higher activity of edge sites than that of planar sites [14]. Therefore, edge-deficient N-doped sites exhibit high ORR activity [15]. Furthermore, most the N-doped carbon catalysts are obtained through the pyrolysis of carbon and nitrogen polymers [16,17]. High-temperature pyrolysis leads to a decrease in the defect and N contents of the materials. Therefore, the preparation of N-doped carbon catalysts with high edge defects through rational design requires further investigation [18].

As a new type of zero-dimensional material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have high electrical conductivity, high specific surface area, and edge-rich defects [19]. GQDs can be utilized as unique structures for the fabrication of functional carbon materials [20], which have been widely used in electrochemistry, photocatalysis, and biology. However, GQDs tend to aggregate into bulk carbon with a low specific surface area via direct carbonization because of their large surface energy, resulting in low catalytic activity [21]. To prevent aggregation, the use of templates is a common strategy for the preparation of carbon-based catalysts from GQDs [22]. GQDs with small sizes, edge effects, and heteroatom doping are promising candidates for metal-free electrocatalysts [23,24].

Herein, the nitrogen-containing coal-based GQDs with good conductivity and edge-rich defects were used as carbon and nitrogen sources. Porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheet (PNCNs) catalysts with rich defects were prepared using an in situ self-doped template method. The introduction of a Mg(OH)2 template results in the formation of catalysts with a loose stacked layer structure, which not only improves the mass transfer process for the ORR but also provides additional active sites. PNCNs obtained at 900 °C (PNCNs-900) showed excellent ORR performance, with E onset = 0.88 V. It also exhibited high stability and methanol resistance, even better than those of Pt/C. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated that graphitic N with defects is highly active for ORR.

KOH (AR, 95%), ethanol (AR, water<0.3%), MgCl2·6 H2O (AR, 98%), and HCl (37 wt%) were bought from Aladdin and used directly without further purification. According to reference [24], coal-based GQDs were prepared by chemical oxidation method. In simple terms, the 100 mL HNO3 (67 wt%) and H2SO4 (98 wt%) were slowly and carefully added into 5 g of the bituminous coal fine powder, reflux at 80 °C for 10 h for oxidation cutting. The product was stirred, centrifuged and washed with a large amount of distilled water. Finally, N-doped coal-based graphene quantum dots were obtained by dialysis.

In typical procedure, 22 g MgCl2·6 H2O dissolved in 150 mL deionized water to prepare solution A. Then 10 g KOH dissolved in 100 mL deionized water to prepare solution B. Solution B was quickly poured into solution A with intensely stirred, reacted for 1 h. Finally, Mg(OH)2 slices were obtained after drying following washed to neutral by deionized water.

In a typical preparation, 1 g of GQDs and 3 g of Mg(OH)2 slices were dispersed in 60 mL of ethanol, and then mixed well by vigorous stirring and sonication. After drying, the solid mixtures were heated at 900 °C for 1 h under Ar atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1 . Etching the template with 1 mol L−1 HCl, PNCNs-900 was finally obtained after washing with deionized water and ethanol, respectively. At different carbonization temperatures, the PNCNs-T (T = pyrolysis temperature) materials were obtained. GQDs and Mg(OH)2 slices in various mass ratios (for example, the PNCNs made by 1 g of the GQDs and x g of the Mg(OH)2 was named as PNCNs-1:x) were used to prepare PNCNs. For contrast, nitrogen-doped carbon (NC-900) was prepared by direct pyrolysis of GQDs without the addition of Mg(OH)2 under the 900 °C.

The morphology of samples was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800 H), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi-600), high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2010 F). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Bruker D8 by Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) with angle range of 10° ≤ 2θ ≤ 80°. The Raman spectra were achieved on a Bruker Senterra Raman spectroscope with 532 nm wave length incident laser light. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained by using a ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer with Al-Ka X-ray source (1486.7 eV). The surface area was obtained by the Brunauere Emmette Teller (BET) method from N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Micro ASAP 2420). The pore size distribution was calculated by the Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method based on the N2 desorption curves.

All the electrochemical measurements for ORR were performed using a CHI 760e electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode system in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution at room temperature. A glassy carbon (GC) rotating-disk electrode (RDE, 5 mm in diameter) was used as the working electrode, platinum and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared as follows. 2.5 mg of the samples was dispersed in 150 μL deionized water, 340 μL ethanol, and 10 μL Nafion solution (5 wt%), then the mixture was ultrasonicated for about 2 h to generate ink. 10 μL ink was dropped on the RDE electrode (the catalyst loading of 0.2547 mg cm−2), and electrochemical measurements were performed after the solvent evaporated.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were received with the potential range of −1–0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH with N2-saturated or O2-saturated. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were obtained from −1–0 V in O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at 10 mV s−1 with a set of rotation rates (400, 625, 900, 1225, 1600, and 2025 rpm). The kinetic current and the number of electron transfer (n) in ORR process was calculated based on Koutecky-Levich (K-L) equations:
(1)

k

l is the measured current density, kinetic current density, and diffusion limited current, respectively. ω is the electrode rotation rate. B can be calculated from an equation as follows:
(2)
B = 0.2

where n is number of transferred electrons for ORR. F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1). C
0 and D
0 is the saturated oxygen concentration (1.21 ×10−6 mol cm−3) and diffusion coefficient of the dissolved oxygen (1.9 ×10−5 cm2 s−1), respectively. V is the kinetic viscosity (0.01 cm2 s−1).

Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE, 5 mm in diameter, the catalyst loading of 0.2104 mg cm−2) was tested at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with rotating speed of 1600 rpm. The number of electron transfer and the yield of peroxide in the ORR reaction process were calculated by the following equations:
(3)

4

where, j
d is disk current density. j
r is ring current density. N is collection coefficient at the ring as 0.37. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at open-circuit potentials by employing an AC voltage with a 5 mV amplitude in a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. The long-term stability of the ORR were tested at a voltage of 0.5 V (vs. reversible hydrogen electrode, RHE) with the rotation speed of 1600 rpm. All potentials measured were calibrated to RHE using the following equation:
(5)
E
RHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.198 + 0.059
pH

DFT calculations were exploited to calculate the energetics of key ORR intermediates that are necessary to understanding ORR mechanism. Now the widely accepted one is dissociative or associative mechanism [25–27]. In this work, the ORR in alkaline electrolytes is considered to associative mechanism:
(6)
O
2
*
+
H
2
O +
e

−
(7)
HOO

(8)

+

+

(9)

−
+
*

In 2004, J. K. Nørskov's team [28] first proposed that the catalytic activity of catalysts is related to the variation of Gibbs free energy (∆G) under different potentials. The free energy diagram is calculated as follows:
(10)
∆ G = ∆ E + ∆
E
ZPE
− ∆ TS + ∆
G
U
where,
∆ G
is the calculated total energy change.
∆
E
ZPE
is the energy change at zero.
∆ TS
is the entropy change. ∆G
U is defined as -eU. The model structures of the three nitrogen species and defect were designed and optimized based on the experimental results.

The synthesis process of PNCNs is illustrated in Scheme 1. GQDs are used to prepare the ORR electrocatalytic materials because their edge-rich defect and high nitrogen content may provide additional active sites for the ORR. Their conjugated carbon framework with a zero-dimensional structure is also beneficial for the construction of a conductive structure. PNCNs were prepared via one-step carbonization using nitrogen-containing GQDs with edge-rich defects, which were mixed with Mg(OH)2 sheets as templates. During pyrolysis, Mg(OH)2 decomposed into MgO. After the removal of MgO via acid etching, a large number of macropores and mesopores were formed, which significantly increased the specific surface area and enhanced the mass transfer process. The loose stacked layer structure could provides additional active sites and increases electrochemical activity of the material.

The HRTEM images of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 1a–c. GQDs are consistent in size, with a diameter of approximately 2–3 nm. Based on the HRTEM images, clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.20 nm corresponding to the (102) lattice of graphene were observed. The SEM images of NC-900 without the Mg(OH)2 templates are shown in Fig. S1, which displays an irregular blocky shapes. Based on Fig. S2, the Mg(OH)2 template exhibited a messy layered structure. As shown in the SEM (Fig. 1d-f) and TEM images (Fig. 1g-h), PNCNs-900 exhibited an interconnected and cluttered nanosheets structure. A large number of mesopores and micropores were also observed in the PNCNs-900 sample (Fig. 1f). From the SEM image and elemental maps shown in Fig. 1i, the C, N, and O elements in PNCNs-900 were evenly distributed. These results indicate that the Mg(OH)2 template successfully induced the formation of nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheets with a high specific surface area and porosity.

XRD was performed to verify the crystalline structures of the synthesized materials. As shown in Fig. 2a, the diffraction peak at 24.0° corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of the disordered carbon. Meanwhile, the inconspicuous diffraction peak at 43.0° belongs to the (101) lattice plane of the graphitized carbon [22,23]. The bands in the Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) located at the 1210 (T-), 1358 (D-), 1500 (D''-), and 1598 (G-) cm−1 correspond to the heteroatoms in the material, A1 g vibrational modes of the sp3 structure, defects, and ordered graphite lattice, respectively. The D band to G band intensity ratio, ID/IG, typically represents the defect density of carbon materials [24,29]. ID/IG values increased from 1.80 (NC-900) to 1.89 (PNCNs-900) along with an increase in the amount of templates. When the carbonization temperature was further increased to 1000 °C, the ID/IG value decreases to 1.79 (Fig. S3), and the defect density of the material also decreased.

The surface areas and pore structures of the samples were further characterized using N2 adsorption-desorption experiments. The isotherms of NC-900 (Fig. 2c) conform to the type-I isotherm, which is one of the main characteristics of microporous materials. Furthermore, there were virtually no mesopores to guarantee the mass transfer process of the reaction. PNCNs-900 exhibits a reversible type-IV isotherm, with rapid adsorption in the low-pressure region and a distinct hysteresis loop in the medium and high relative pressure regions owing to the presence of micropores and mesopores in the samples. The specific surface area of PNCNs-900 was calculated to be 1355.0 m2 g−1 with a micropore volume of 183.3 cm3 g−1 ( Table 1). The pore-size distribution (Fig. 2d) shows that both NC-900 and PNCNs-900 contain a large number of micropores. The introduction of the Mg(OH)2 template resulted in the formation of abundant mesopores and macropores in PNCNs-900, which would ensured an efficient mass transfer process during the reaction.

XPS was used to analyze the surface composition and chemical states of the materials. As shown in Fig. 3a, XPS spectra of the samples indicated the presence of C, N, and O, which is in accordance with the above element mapping results (Fig. 1i). The N and O contents of the carbonized samples at different temperatures (Fig. 3b) indicate that with an increase in the carbonization temperature, the N content gradually decreased. The XPS C 1 s spectra (Fig. S4) show the chemical states on the surface of the materials, which can be divided into five peaks corresponding to C–C (284.8 eV), C–N (285.4 eV), C–O (286.6 eV), CO (288.4 eV) and π–π * (290.1 eV) [30–32]. Oxygen species were composed of CO (530.9.6 eV), C–O (532.1 eV), and –COO (533.0 eV), as indicated in the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3d). The successful doping of N atoms into the carbon skeleton was confirmed using the corresponding N 1 s spectrum. Based on the N 1 s spectra of the NC-900 and PNCNs-T (Fig. 3c and Figs. S5b), nitrogen species can be classified into pyridine N (398.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV) and graphitic N (400.9 eV). The contents of the three nitrogen species are shown in Fig. 3e. Compared to NC-900, PNCNs-900 had a higher graphitic N content. In addition, the graphitic N content increased with the increasing carbonization temperature. Generally, graphitic N, as a highly active site, can improve the ORR performance [33,34].

The elemental analysis results for the samples ( Table 2) show that NCs-900 contains 87.67% carbon, 4% nitrogen, and 6% oxygen. These results indicate that with an increase in temperature, the N and O content decreases. Based on the XPS and elemental analysis results, we assume that certain amounts of O and N atoms are located in the carbon skeleton [35], which may improve the adsorption capacity of the catalyst for ORR intermediate species.

The ORR reduction activity of the samples was tested via CV in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 4a, compared with the curve under N2-saturation, PNCNs-900 exhibited a distinct oxygen reduction peak in the O2-saturated electrolyte solution. The LSV curves of PNCNs-1:x (x = 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4) are shown in Fig. S6. The half-wave potential (E 1/2 = 0.80 V) of PNCNs-1:3 was more positive than those of the samples with other ratios (1:2 and 1:4), indicating its excellent ORR catalytic activity. LSV measurements (Fig. 4b) of the samples prepared at different temperatures (800, 900, and 1000 °C) were also performed. The half-wave potential of NC-900 is 0.44 V, which is 370 mV more negative than that of PNCNs-900, indicating that the carbon sheet formed by the Mg(OH)2 template is conducive to the exposure of active sites. As shown in Fig. 4c, PNCNs-900 displayed a higher half-wave potential (E 1/2 = 0.80 V) and limiting current density (j = 4.7 mA cm−2) than PNCNs-800 (E 1/2 = 0.75 V, j = 4.6 mA cm−2) and PNCNs-1000 (E 1/2 = 0.74 V, j = 4.4 mA cm−2). Furthermore, the ORR catalytic activity of the samples was evaluated via LSV in 0.1 mol L−1 HClO4 electrolyte using RDE, as shown in Fig. S7, in which PNCNs-900 exhibited the best performance. This implies that the graphitic N content in PNCNs-900 is higher (graphitic N in the acidic electrolyte is highly stable) than those in the other samples, which is consistent with the XPS results. To evaluate the kinetic rate of the materials, Tafel slopes (Fig. 4d) of PNCNs and Pt/C were calculated. The reaction kinetic rate of PNCNs-900 (95 mV dec−1) was slightly slower than that of Pt/C (82 mV dec−1). These results indicate that the synergistic effect between abundant defects and graphitic N in PNCNs-900 is the main reason for the increased catalytic performance [36,37], in accordance with the Raman and XPS results.

The four-electron process of PNCNs-900 in the ORR and electron transfer number (n) can be determined using the K-L equation. Fig. 4e shows the LSV curves obtained at different rotational speeds. According to the K-L equation [38,39], the electron transfer number was calculated. As shown in the Fig. 4g, the curves exhibit a good correlation. The n was calculated to be 3.79. The electrochemical specific surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst was obtained from the measured CV curves in the non-Faradaic region at different scanning rates (Fig. S8). Up on the introduction of the Mg(OH)2 template, the ECSA of the catalyst increased substantially, indicating that the electrocatalytic activity of PNCNs-900 was significantly improved. EIS (Fig. S9) further confirmed that the template agent successfully induced the formation of a continuous conductive network, which significantly enhanced the conductivity of the catalyst. In summary, a continuous conductive network, effective doping, and abundant defects play important roles in producing excellent interface dynamics of electrodes.

The RRDE ( Fig. 5a) was employed to determine the H2O2 yield [40–42] and the n values of the catalysts from the experimental investigations. The results (Fig. 5b-c) show that the n for PNCNs-900 is 3.80, which is consistent with the calculation results obtained using the K-L equation in the potential range of 0.2–0.8 V. In addition, PNCNs-900 showed similar n values as Pt/C (n = 3.92), which further proved the occurrence of a four-electron reaction in PNNCs-900. Meanwhile, the H2O2 yield of PNCNs-900 was only 10.08%. The long-term cyclic stability of a catalyst is another important parameter for evaluating its performance. The stability of the PNCNs-900 sample for 5 h at a voltage of 0.5 V was characterized using chronoamperometry (CA). The resulting i-t diagram (Fig. 5d) of PNCNs-900 reveals excellent stability with 84% current retention after 5 h, which is better than that of Pt/C (73% current retention). After 5 h of cycling, the half-wave potential of PNCNs-900 shifted from 35 mV to negative values (Fig. 5e). To test the methanol tolerance of PNCNs-900 and Pt/C, 1 mol L−1 methanol was added to the electrolyte solution. After 1 h, PNCNs-900 showed excellent methanol tolerance compared to Pt/C. As shown in Fig. 5f, PNCNs-900 exhibited 89% current retention after 1 h, whereas the current of Pt/C remained only 75%. Therefore, PNCNs-900 showed good four-electron oxygen reduction activity as well as better cycle stability and methanol tolerance than Pt/C.

DFT calculations were used to study the free energy changes of the reaction intermediates and investigate the reactivity of defects with the N species at the interface. The optimized structures of edge C coupled with pyridinic N (N1/C), edge C coupled with pyrrolic N (N2/C), planar C coupled with graphitic N (N3/C), and edge C coupled with graphitic N (N4/C) are shown in Fig. 6a. The C atoms adjacent to N were used as the active sites to calculate the free energy of the ORR processes (Fig. S10). In the step diagram for U = 1.23 V, the ORR free energy diagrams in Fig. 6c show that the formation of OOH* and OH* species at the four active sites is endothermic. For the N4/C active site, the generation of OH* (0.92 eV is required) was the slowest step and represented the highest resistance for the entire ORR. However, the slowest step in the N4/C active site (0.92 eV) is lower than those in N3/C (1.49 eV), N2/C (1.21 eV), and N1/C (1.80 eV) as the other three active sites required higher energy to generate OOH* . In summary, the reaction energy barrier of the N4/C structure was lower than those of the other three structures. Simultaneously, we analyzed the total density of states (TDOSs) of different N species. As shown in Fig. S11, TDOS overlap of graphitic N is higher than that those of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N at the Fermi level (0 eV), which indicates the high electrical conductivity and easy charge transfer of graphitic N. Therefore, the ORR catalytic activity of PNCNs-900 was enhanced by the presence of defects in the graphitic N sites [43,44], which is consistent with the electrochemical measurements.

In this study, benefitting from the rich edge defects in nitrogen-containing coal-based GQDs, we prepared PNCNs with large surface areas using a straightforward template-assisted assembly strategy. The loose stacked-layer structure of the PNCNs can improve the mass transfer process of the ORR. PNCNs-900 had the highest defect density and graphitic N content and exhibited outstanding ORR activity. Furthermore, DFT calculations confirmed that the carbon site adjacent to the graphitic N in PNCNs-900 was a highly active site for ORR. Based on the results, PNCNs-900 exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity owing to the synergistic effect of abundant defects and graphitic N. This study provides a simple method for the preparation of electrocatalysts from coal-based carbon materials and offers new ideas for the utilization of coal resources.

J. Lei and Y. Li conceived and designed the study. J. Lei and K. Wang performed the experiments. B. Deng performed the simulation calculations. J. Lei and Y. Li wrote the manuscript. S. Zhang and Y. Cao reviewed and edited the manuscript. All authors have given their approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21766036, 52062046, and 52100166).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.165359.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
